title: What is PCA, Really?
concept: Principal Component Analysis (PCA) finds the directions in your data that capture the most variation and keeps only those, discarding the rest.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What does dimensionality reduction do to a dataset?

options.text: Compresses many features down to a smaller set that keeps the important signal
Groups similar data points into clusters
Predicts a category label for each row

exercises.explain: Right! Dimensionality reduction compresses many features into fewer, more useful ones. Now let's meet its most famous algorithm: PCA.

cards.title: The best angle for a shadow puppet
cards.body: Shine a flashlight on your hand shaped like a bird, and the wall shows a clear, recognizable bird silhouette — but only from certain angles. Twist your hand the wrong way and the shadow turns into a shapeless blob. PCA is about finding that "best angle" mathematically, for data instead of shadows.

cards.title: What is PCA?
cards.body: Principal Component Analysis (PCA) is the most widely used dimensionality reduction algorithm. It examines a dataset's features and finds new directions — built by combining the original features — that capture the most variation. It keeps the top few of these directions and drops the rest.

cards.title: Flattening a crumpled ball of paper
cards.body: Press a crumpled ball of paper flat one way and you get a big, recognizable sheet. Press it a different way and it crushes into a tiny wrinkled dot. PCA finds the "press direction" for your data that keeps it as spread out and recognizable as possible, instead of crushing away what matters.

cards.title: Simplifying fitness tracker data
cards.body: A fitness app tracks 12 daily metrics: steps, heart rate, sleep hours, calories, and more. PCA can discover that two new combined directions — roughly "Overall Activity Level" and "Sleep Quality" — capture almost all the meaningful differences between days, without needing to look at all 12 numbers separately.

cards.title: PCA doesn't just pick your favorite original features
cards.body: It's tempting to think PCA selects a handful of your existing columns (like square footage or age) and throws the rest away. In reality, PCA builds brand-new features that are blends of the originals — a principal component might be "70% square footage + 30% number of bedrooms," a column that never existed in the raw data.

cards.title: Feature Selection vs. PCA
cards.body: Two different ways to simplify a dataset:

left.text:
Keeps a subset of the original columns
unchanged

right.text:
Builds brand-new combined columns
blended from the originals

exercises.prompt: What does PCA search for in a dataset?

options.text: New directions that capture the most variation in the data
The single most expensive feature to collect
Groups of similar rows to cluster together

exercises.explain: Right! PCA searches for new directions — combinations of the original features — that capture the most variation.

exercises.prompt: True or false: PCA keeps a subset of your original features exactly as they were, unchanged.
exercises.explain: False! PCA builds brand-new combined features rather than just keeping a subset of the originals untouched.

exercises.sentence: PCA builds brand-new features that are ___ of the original features, not just a subset of them.
exercises.explain: PCA's new features are combinations (blends) of the original ones — not copies or labels.

options.text: A retailer with 40 tracked product attributes builds 3 new combined "super-features" that capture almost all the meaningful differences between products
A retailer manually picks its 5 favorite product attributes and deletes the rest
A retailer groups similar products into 5 categories based on their attributes

exercises.explain: Correct! Building new combined features that capture the most variation is exactly what PCA does — not manual picking, and not grouping rows into categories.

exercises.prompt: A fashion app tracks 80 attributes per clothing item (color, fabric, pattern, sleeve length, and more). A teammate suggests "just pick our 5 favorite attributes and ignore the rest" to simplify the data. Why might PCA be the better choice?

options.text: PCA builds new combined features engineered to capture the most variation across all 80 attributes, rather than relying on a guess about which 5 matter most
PCA guarantees the model will never make a mistake
PCA works by grouping similar clothing items into clusters
PCA and manually picking 5 favorite attributes always produce identical results

exercises.explain: Exactly! PCA systematically builds combined features that capture the most variation across every attribute, instead of relying on a manual guess about which few matter most.
